chapter_info.subject: Social Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Socialism in Europe and the Russian Revolution

flashcards.q: What did liberals believe about voting rights?
flashcards.a: Liberals did not believe in universal adult franchise. They felt only men of property should have the vote and did not want women to have voting rights.

flashcards.q: What was the suffragette movement?
flashcards.a: A movement to give women the right to vote. Radicals supported it while liberals opposed it.

flashcards.q: What did radicals oppose?
flashcards.a: Radicals opposed the privileges of great landowners and wealthy factory owners. They disliked the concentration of property in the hands of a few.

flashcards.q: What was the conservatives' position on change after the French Revolution?
flashcards.a: They accepted that some change was inevitable but believed the past had to be respected and change should be brought about through a slow process.

flashcards.q: Who was Robert Owen?
flashcards.a: A leading English manufacturer who sought to build a cooperative community called New Harmony in Indiana, USA.

flashcards.q: What did Louis Blanc propose?
flashcards.a: He wanted the government to encourage cooperatives and replace capitalist enterprises. Cooperatives would be associations of people who produced goods together and divided profits according to work done.

flashcards.q: According to Karl Marx, what was a communist society?
flashcards.a: A radically socialist society where all property was socially controlled. Marx believed it was the natural society of the future where workers would triumph over capitalists.

flashcards.q: What was the Second International?
flashcards.a: An international body formed by socialists in the 1870s to coordinate their efforts across Europe.

flashcards.q: What was the Paris Commune remembered for?
flashcards.a: Two legacies: its association with the workers' red flag, and the 'Marseillaise' as a symbol of the Commune and struggle for liberty.

flashcards.q: Who was Tsar Nicholas II?
flashcards.a: The Tsar who ruled Russia and its empire in 1914. He abdicated on 2 March 1917 during the February Revolution.

flashcards.q: What proportion of Russia's population earned their living from agriculture?
flashcards.a: About 85 per cent, higher than most European countries.

flashcards.q: What was the mir in Russian villages?
flashcards.a: The peasant commune that pooled land together periodically and divided it according to the needs of individual families.

flashcards.q: What was the Socialist Revolutionary Party?
flashcards.a: A party formed in 1900 that struggled for peasants' rights and demanded that land belonging to nobles be transferred to peasants.

flashcards.q: What was Bloody Sunday?
flashcards.a: 22 January 1905, when a peaceful procession of workers was fired upon at the Winter Palace, killing over 100 and wounding about 300, sparking the 1905 Revolution.

flashcards.q: What was the Duma?
flashcards.a: An elected consultative parliament allowed by the Tsar during the 1905 Revolution. The Tsar dismissed the first two Dumas and packed the third with conservatives.

flashcards.q: Why was St Petersburg renamed Petrograd?
flashcards.a: Due to anti-German sentiments during the First World War, as St Petersburg was a German name.

flashcards.q: What happened on 27 February 1917 in Petrograd?
flashcards.a: Police Headquarters were ransacked, and soldiers and striking workers formed the Petrograd Soviet in the same building as the Duma.

flashcards.q: When did Tsar Nicholas II abdicate?
flashcards.a: 2 March 1917, after military commanders advised him to do so following the February Revolution.

flashcards.q: What were Lenin's three demands in the 'April Theses'?
flashcards.a: That the war be brought to a close, land be transferred to peasants, and banks be nationalised.

flashcards.q: When did the October Revolution begin?
flashcards.a: 24 October 1917 (7 November by Gregorian calendar), with the uprising led by the Military Revolutionary Committee under Leon Trotskii.

flashcards.q: What was the Brest Litovsk treaty?
flashcards.a: The peace treaty signed in March 1918 between Bolshevik Russia and Germany, ending Russia's participation in the First World War despite opposition from political allies.

flashcards.q: What was the Cheka?
flashcards.a: The secret police established by the Bolsheviks to punish those who criticised the government. It was later renamed OGPU and NKVD.

flashcards.q: Who fought whom in the Russian Civil War?
flashcards.a: The 'Reds' (Bolsheviks) fought against the 'Whites' (pro-Tsarists) and the 'Greens' (Socialist Revolutionaries), with foreign troops from France, America, Britain and Japan supporting the opposition.

flashcards.q: When was the Soviet Union (USSR) formed?
flashcards.a: December 1922, created by the Bolsheviks from the former Russian empire.

flashcards.q: What were the Five Year Plans?
flashcards.a: Centralised economic plans where officials set targets for five-year periods to promote industrial growth in Soviet Russia.

flashcards.q: What were kulaks?
flashcards.a: The name for well-to-do peasants targeted during collectivisation as alleged hoarders of grain.

flashcards.q: What were kolkhoz?
flashcards.a: Collective farms in Soviet Russia where peasants were forced to cultivate from 1929 onwards, with land and implements transferred to collective ownership.

flashcards.q: What happened to cattle during collectivisation?
flashcards.a: The number of cattle fell by one-third between 1929 and 1931 as enraged peasants destroyed their livestock rather than hand it over to collective farms.

flashcards.q: What was the Comintern?
flashcards.a: An international union of pro-Bolshevik socialist parties founded by the Bolsheviks to coordinate communist movements worldwide.

flashcards.q: What was the budenovka?
flashcards.a: The distinctive Soviet hat designed for the army and officials after a clothing competition in 1918 to assert the new socialist identity.

flashcards.q: What was the difference between Bolsheviks and Mensheviks regarding party organisation?
flashcards.a: Bolsheviks (led by Lenin) believed the party should be disciplined with controlled membership; Mensheviks believed it should be open to all.

flashcards.q: What happened to the Constituent Assembly elected in November 1917?
flashcards.a: It rejected Bolshevik measures in January 1918, and Lenin dismissed it, arguing the All Russian Congress of Soviets was more democratic.

flashcards.q: What were jadidists?
flashcards.a: Muslim reformers within the Russian empire who wanted modernised Islam to lead their society.

flashcards.q: What is meant by 'real wages'?
flashcards.a: The quantities of goods that wages will actually buy, reflecting the true purchasing power of earnings.
